beats.text: Hyperparameters are the settings fixed before training, such as learning rate, tree depth, and regularization strength.
Tuning is an optimization problem: find the configuration that minimizes the cross-validated loss over the search space.

visual.note: f is a black box, and each query trains the model k times

beats.text: The objective is a black box with no gradient, and every evaluation is expensive because one query means k model fits.
Learning rate spans orders of magnitude, so you sample it log-uniformly, giving each decade from ten to the minus five upward equal attention.

visual.note: geometric center of a log-uniform range

beats.text: A full grid is the product of per-axis counts, so ten values across six hyperparameters is already a million configurations.
Of the dozen hyperparameters you nominally tune, usually only two or three actually move the validation score.

concepts.title: Grid search: exhaustive and quietly wasteful

beats.text: Grid search picks finite values per hyperparameter, takes the Cartesian product, evaluates every point, and keeps the best.
Refining a grid from five to ten values per axis multiplies the cost by two to the power of D, so resolution gets exponentially coarser.
A g by g grid makes g-squared evaluations but tests only g distinct values of the one axis that actually matters.

visual.tex: g^{D} \text{ evaluations}, \quad \text{but only } g \text{ distinct values of the axis that matters}
visual.note: the rest of the budget repeats unimportant combinations

beats.text: In the runnable example a six by six grid spends thirty-six evaluations yet probes only six distinct values of the dominant x axis.
Grid search stays defensible for one or two cheap hyperparameters, or where an auditable even sweep matters in regulated settings.

visual.label: hyperparameters where a small grid is clear and defensible

beats.text: Random search replaces the grid with independent draws from each hyperparameter's distribution for a fixed budget of trials.
Bergstra and Bengio showed in 2012 that at equal budget random search matches or beats grid search on neural-network tuning.
With low effective dimensionality random search spends its whole budget on many distinct values of the few axes that matter.
The coverage guarantee says n independent draws miss the top p fraction of the space with probability one minus p, raised to n.

visual.tex: \Pr[\text{at least one trial in top } p] = 1 - (1-p)^{n} \;\Longrightarrow\; n \geq \frac{\ln(1-c)}{\ln(1-p)}
visual.note: independent of how many hyperparameters you have

beats.text: To hit the top five percent of configurations with ninety-five percent confidence you need only fifty-nine random trials.

visual.label: trials to reach top 5% with 95% confidence

beats.text: That bound is dimension-free: it depends only on how good good enough is, not on the number of hyperparameters.

concepts.title: Bayesian optimization: spend queries where they pay

beats.text: Grid and random search are uninformed; Bayesian optimization closes the loop by learning from the trials seen so far.
It fits a cheap probabilistic surrogate of the objective, then uses it to choose the most promising next query.
A Gaussian process returns a posterior mean and a standard deviation, which is large exactly where you have not yet looked.
The confidence-bound acquisition picks the lowest mean minus kappa times sigma, rewarding both low predicted loss and high uncertainty.
For mean two and sigma zero point two, kappa equals two gives an acquisition value of one point six for minimization.

visual.label: acquisition value for mu=2.0, sigma=0.2, kappa=2

beats.text: Gaussian-process surrogates scale poorly past a couple dozen dimensions, so modern tuners pair them with an early-stopping scheduler.
For iterative learners a configuration's fate is often visible early, so you need not run every candidate to completion.
Successive halving starts many configs on a tiny budget, discards the worst fraction, and gives survivors more budget each round.
Survivors fall geometrically while per-config budget rises geometrically, so total budget spent per round stays roughly constant.

visual.tex: n_r = \Big\lfloor \frac{n_0}{\eta^{r}} \Big\rfloor, \quad b_r = b_0\,\eta^{r}, \quad n_r\, b_r \approx n_0\, b_0
visual.note: eta is the cull factor, usually 3

beats.text: With eta three and twenty-seven starting configs, the bracket reaches a single survivor in exactly three elimination rounds.
Hyperband refuses to choose one bracket and instead hedges across a spectrum of config-versus-budget trade-offs.
Hyperband loses only a small logarithmic factor to the best fixed bracket chosen in hindsight, the basis of today's ASHA scheduler.
